Lookup Fields: UCCONTRACT , CACONT_ACC

Different ways to lookup fields from another internal tables are: 

1. Lookup using Join:

inTab is joined (LEFT OUTER JOIN) with table /BI0/PUCCONTRACT with ucinslalla as key field. This way we get access to all the fields that are present in table /BI0/PUCCONTRACT. 

Note: we can derive other fields based on conditional logic, string operation, constant, etc.  

 

outTab = SELECT
        T1.DATEFROM,
        T1.UCINSTALLA,
        T1.UCOPERAND,
        T1.UC_SEASON,
        T1.RECORDMODE,
        T1."/BIC/ZBILLDOC",
        T1."/BIC/ZWERT1",
        (CASE WHEN T1."/BIC/ZSTRING1" IS NULL THEN 'X' ELSE T1."/BIC/ZSTRING1" END) AS "/BIC/ZSTRING1",
        (CASE WHEN T1."/BIC/ZSTRING2" IS NULL THEN '' ELSE LEFT(T1."/BIC/ZSTRING2",3) END) AS "/BIC/ZSTRING2",
        T1."/BIC/ZSTRING3",
        T1."/BIC/ZSTRING4",
        T1."/BIC/ZERSAT",
        T1."/BIC/ZWERT2",
        '99991231' AS DATETO,
        1 AS FC_COUNT,
        T1.UCRATEFAKT,
        T1.UCRATE_TY,
        T1.UNIT,
        T1.VAR_VR,
        T1.CURRENCY,
        T2.CACONT_ACC,
        T2.UCCONTRACT,
        T1.RECORD,
        T1.SQL__PROCEDURE__SOURCE__RECORD
    FROM :inTab AS T1 
    LEFT OUTER JOIN "/BI0/PUCCONTRACT" AS T2 ON T1.UCINSTALLA = T2.UCINSTALLA;

 

 

2. Lookup using nested Loop

Iterate on target table variable (outTab using index i) and then iterate lookup table variable (caTab using index j), assign lookup fields where the key UCINSTALLA matches in both tables.  

Note: Nested loops are useful when there are multiple records in lookup table for every record in target table. Nested loop is performance intensive.

 

    DECLARE i, j, lv_count, lv_cacount INTEGER;

-- Direct assignment of inTab to outTab
    outTab = SELECT * FROM :inTab;
-- Counting number of records in outTab using RECORD_COUNT function
    lv_count = RECORD_COUNT(:outTab);

-- Lookup SELECT to get CACONT_ACC & UCCONTRACT from table /BI0/PUCCONTRACT for each UCINSTALLA in outTab into table variable caTab
    caTab = SELECT UCINSTALLA, CACONT_ACC, UCCONTRACT FROM "/BI0/PUCCONTRACT"
            WHERE UCINSTALLA IN ( SELECT DISTINCT UCINSTALLA FROM :outTab) ORDER BY UCINSTALLA;
    SELECT COUNT(*) INTO lv_cacount FROM :caTab; -- Counting number of records in caTab using query

-- Initialise both varibales i & j to 0.
    i = 0; j = 0;

   FOR i IN 1..lv_count DO -- Can use RECORD_COUNT(:outTab) directly instead of variable lv_count
            FOR j in 1..:lv_cacount DO
-- If ucinstalla of outTab matches with ucinstalla of caTab then assign lookup fields uccontract & cacont_acc
                IF :outTab.ucinstalla[i] = :caTab.ucinstalla[j] THEN
                    outTab.uccontract[i] = :caTab.uccontract[j];
                    outTab.cacont_acc[i] = :caTab.cacont_acc[j];
                END IF;
            END FOR;
    END FOR;

 

 

3. Lookup using SEARCH function

SEARCH function is similar to READ statement in ABAP. Finds the search value and returns the position (index) in table variable.   

 

DECLARE i, j, lv_count, lv_cacount INTEGER;

-- Direct assignment of inTab to outTab
    outTab = SELECT * FROM :inTab;
-- Counting number of records in outTab using RECORD_COUNT function
    lv_count = RECORD_COUNT(:outTab);

-- Lookup SELECT to get CACONT_ACC & UCCONTRACT from table /BI0/PUCCONTRACT for each UCINSTALLA in outTab into table variable caTab
    caTab = SELECT UCINSTALLA, CACONT_ACC, UCCONTRACT FROM "/BI0/PUCCONTRACT"
            WHERE UCINSTALLA IN ( SELECT DISTINCT UCINSTALLA FROM :outTab) ORDER BY UCINSTALLA;
    SELECT COUNT(*) INTO lv_cacount FROM :caTab; -- Counting number of records in caTab using query

-- Initialise both varibales i & j to 0.
    i = 0; j = 0;

    FOR i IN 1..RECORD_COUNT(:outTab) DO -- Using RECORD_COUNT function directly without variable
        j = :caTab.SEARCH(ucinstalla, :outTab.ucinstalla[i]);
        IF j <> 0 THEN -- if search fails j will have value 0, which may cause error. So always check for value of j
            outTab.uccontract[i] = :caTab.uccontract[j];
            outTab.cacont_acc[i] = :caTab.cacont_acc[j];
        ELSE
            outTab.cacont_acc[i] = '';
            outTab.uccontract[i] = '';
        END IF;
    END FOR;

 

 

Conclusion:

Based on the requirement the lookup can be performed in HANA routine similar to ABAP routine. It is important to choose the right approach:

Direct relationship exists -> use JOIN 

Multiple rows exists in lookup table for each row in target table -> use Nested loop

Single row exists in   lookup table for each row in target table -> use SEARCH function

entry.title: Planning to Deploy SAP BW/4HANA 2023? Understand the Limitations and Possibilities for SAP BW 7.5.
entry.content:
Hi,

Greetings!

Are you planning for a Conversion to SAPBW/4HANA 2023, from SAP BW 7.5, please check out few notes to consider:

Pre-requisite for SAP BW7.50 version:

 

Incase already on SAP BW/4HANA 1.0, follow the simple and smooth 2 step Upgrade:

Step-1) SAP BW/4HANA 1.0 -> SAP BW/4HANA 2.0 -> SAP BW/4HANA 2023
Step-2) SAP BW/4HANA 1.0 -> SAP BW/4HANA 2021 -> SAP BW/4HANA 2023

Please note - Currently direct Upgrade from SAP BW 1.0 to SAP BW/4HANA 2023 is not possible!!!

Incase if you are planning any of the existing Conversion approaches based upon the existing SAP BW Version(SAP BW 7.x to BW4HANA 2021 or BW4HANA 2023), whether it is REMOTE or SHELL or In-Place, a few findings from my experience are listed for your reference before starting the actual Realization Phase of any Conversion:

1)Consider the Discovery Phase as knowledge platform, work closely with SAP-BASIS to implement Maintenance Planner.

2)Note down the findings of Readiness Check and Simplification List.

3)Align with the Upcoming /Planned Source-System Upgrades/Migrations.

4)Define the scope of Business Applications and carve out the unwanted flows.

5)Cleanup the Obsolete Source Systems along with the dependent Objects which are persistent in the system

6)Create a Production Copy of BW System and try to simulate the Conversion which gives more insights on the functionalities which are replaced with new Simplified Info Providers, Data Extraction Platform (ODP), Reporting Tools, Modelling Tools et.,

7)Perform data Consistency checks by leveraging the SAP Delivered Programs to handle the repairing of Master Data and SIDs, note down Listed Objects, this will help to analyze and get well-prepared for handling the actual data migration in Production Landscape.

8)Do the ABAP Code Compatibility Checks which can be initiated from Conversion Cockpit. This can be done multiple times to ensure the Sender System is ready for Conversion.

9)Go for periodic system Backups at various phases of Conversion.

10)List out the incompatible Extractors and enable the marker for ODP Extraction.

Consider the findings from Discovery Phase and carry forward the fixes, finding and alternative workarounds based upon the customization factor of data models and code, then will help to succeed the Transformation and plan the downtime of Production during Go-Live.

HAPPY Conversion! Get ready to enable Real-time Data Processing, Simplified modeling and Administration with prefabricated templates.

 

Many Thanks,

Sunil Babu Sanjamala

entry.title: Re-initializing of deltas in BW4HANA from S4HANA (For error Subscription for DTP* deleted in source)
entry.content:
Overview:

Re-initializing deltas between S4HANA and BW4HANA is vital for maintaining precise data synchronization. This process entails removing the existing initialization request in BW4HANA, purging previous data from the data targets, and establishing a new initialization. The error message indicates that the subscription for the Data Transfer Process (DTP) has been removed in the source system (S4HANA).

To address this issue, examine the DTP settings in BW4HANA, recreate the subscription if necessary, review system logs for further details, and re-initialize the delta process.

Error Occurred in BW4HANA System:

Subscription for DTP* deleted in source system. The error is due to deletion on subscription in S4 side or the subscription of DTP is mapped to wrong source system.

 

Check ODQMON by putting data source name in queue and see the subscriptions. 

 

Solution to the error:

Resolution 1:

Step 1) To delete a subscription in T-Code ODQMON in S4HANA, open ODQMON, select the DataSource, go to the "Subscriptions" tab, and delete the desired subscription. Confirm the deletion by checking the "Queues" tab to ensure the request is inactive. 

 

Step 2) To initiate a new subscription from BW4HANA to S4HANA, it is essential to delete all existing requests in the target system. If these requests are not removed, you will encounter errors. Therefore, ensure that all pending requests are cleared before proceeding with the new subscription setup. This step is crucial to avoid any interruptions or issues during the process.

Before deleting all the requests in BW side, ensure that you have created a copy of your DSO, ADSO or info objects.

Open the monitor in SAP GUI

Change overall Status to RED 

And delete the requests one by one. Please ensure all the requests are deleted from the target before moving to next step.

Step 3) To begin, manually initiate the Data Transfer Process (DTP) in delta mode using Eclipse. After starting the DTP, check the ODQMON transaction in your S4HANA system to confirm that a new subscription has been created. This verification step is crucial to ensure that the data transfer setup is functioning correctly.

 

Note** If you continue to encounter errors on the BW4HANA side after completing the previous steps, please run the report RODPS_REPL_TEST in transaction SE38 for the data source on the S4HANA side. This report will help diagnose and resolve any remaining issues with the data transfer process.

Resolution 2:

     Deleting the existing DTP

  • Identify the DTP: Locate the Data Transfer Process (DTP) associated with the info provider you want to update.
  • Remove from Process Chains: Ensure that the DTP is removed from all related process chains to avoid any disruptions.
  • Delete the DTP: Once removed from the process chains, delete the DTP from the system.    

     

      Creating a New Delta DTP

  • Create the Delta DTP: Set up a new delta DTP in BW. This DTP will handle the incremental data loads.
  • Run Initialization Without Data: Execute the initialization process without transferring any data. This step is crucial as it creates a new subscription in S4HANA.
  • Monitor in ODQMON: Check the Operational Delta Queue Monitor (ODQMON) in S4HANA to ensure that new delta records are being captured correctly.

    

     Handling Potential Data Loss

  • Create a Repair DTP: If there's a risk of data loss, create a Repair DTP to perform a full data load. This ensures that all data is transferred accurately.
  • Compare Data Volumes: For large data volumes, compare the data in BW and S4HANA to identify any discrepancies or missing records.
  • Load Missing Data: Use the Repair DTP with specific selections to load the missing data into BW. This step ensures data consistency between BW and S4HANA.

     Additional Tips

  • Regular Monitoring: Continuously monitor the data transfer process to catch any issues early.
  • Backup Data: Always have a backup of your data before making significant changes to the DTPs.
  • Documentation: Keep detailed documentation of the changes made for future reference and troubleshooting

 

By following these steps, you can effectively manage your data transfer processes in SAP BW and S4HANA, ensuring data integrity and minimizing the risk of data loss.

entry.title: Leveraging SAP Integration Suite for Salesforce extraction into SAP BW/4HANA
entry.content:
*This article has been collaboratively created with Frank Riesner .

Dear BW enthusiasts!
There have been several contributions covering the ADSO write-interface already, like
Data Integration with SAP BW/4HANA
SAP Cloud Integration into SAP BW/4HANA
SAP BW/4HANA write interface-enabled ADSO connected to a 3rd Party Tool
SAP Cloud Platform Integration (CPI) to push data into SAP BW/4HANA

There are various approaches to connect Salesfore as a source with the aforementioned write-interface of SAP BW/4HANA:

  1. Via SAP HANA Smart Data Integration and its OData Adapter.
    (SAP Note: 2776343 describes the approach and same Interface for SAP SuccessFactors. However is is a useful source to understand the general approach for 3rd Party Products like Salesforce as well regarding the interface towards BW) 
  2. Via SAP Data Services and its Salesforce Adapter or the generic OData Adapter.
  3. Via SAP BTP Integration Suite and the Salesforce Adapter
    (SAP Help, SAP Note: 3001980 and this Blog provide a more general overview under the aspect of "Non-SAP Integration - New Version of Adapters and Integration Packs)  

In this Blog, we dive deep into approach (3) based on the SAP BTP Integration Suite. The intention is to give you a detailed approach for the specific use case to load data from Salesforce to SAP BW/4HANA.

Prerequisites:

  • In SAP BW/4HANA system there is an DataStore object (ADSO) available with the special property “Write Interface enabled". After activation of this new ADSO, template URIs (Uniform Resource Identifiers) are generated in the Properties window by default. These provide the target interface for our scenario. For more details refer to the SAP Help ADSO modeling properties (note: This feature is not available in BW 7.5).
  • In SAP Integration Suite the Salesforce Adapter has been activated and is available.
  • Credentials and Access Tokens for Salesforce and BW has been generated.


The target interface in SAP BW/4HANA is represented by following URIs:
In this table below, the ADSO technical name is represented by term "ZADSO". In the illustrations our template ADSO is called "zc8sfop01".

DescriptionHTTP MethodURLIllustration

Get all ADSOs and Request XCSRF-Token

GET https://server:8443/sap/bw4/v1/push/dataStores/ 

Get structure and meta data of specific ADSO (here called "ZADSO")

GEThttps://server:8443/sap/bw4/v1/push/dataStores/zadso
Get sample Data e.g. 10 examplesGEThttps://server:8443/sap/bw4/v1/push/dataStores/zadso/sampleData?records=10&seed=0
Send Data without TSN POSThttps://server:8443/sap/bw4/v1/push/dataStores/zadso/dataSend 
Create New TSN request (Transaction Sequence Number) POSThttps://server:8443/sap/bw4/v1/push/dataStores/zadso/requests 
Send Data with TSN POST

https://server:8443/sap/bw4/v1/push/dataStores/zadso/dataSend?request=[tsn]&datapid=[dynamic starting at 1 + n]

Close Request for specific TSN POSThttps://server:8443/sap/bw4/v1/push/dataStores/zc8sfop01/requests/[tsn]/close 

 

Step-by-Step - Process description:

  1. Recover the timestamp of the last run as the data load runs once a day.
  2. Perform a SOQL query to Salesforce, including the timestamp of the last run, to collect all new entries.
  3. Check for new entries; otherwise, cancel further processing.
  4. Perform a GET to fetch the X-CSRF-Token, save the token and cookies.
  5. Perform a POST to request a new TSN for data transfer and save the TSN.
  6. Perform message mapping with Groovy script from Salesforce result to SAP BW/4HANA target structure.
  7. Perform a POST with SAP BW/4HANA payload, including TSN in the URL.
  8. Perform a POST to close the related TSN request.
  9. If successful, save the new timestamp of the last run.

Design Time details in BTP Integration Suite

StepIllustration

Main Flow 

Remember last execution of data load and last TSN ID

Get Content from Salesforce 

Get X-CSRF-Token 

Send data to SAP BW/4HANA

 

Get source data from Salesforce 

Data Load from Salesforce with SOQL Query 

Get Token

Request X-CSRF-Token with Fetch  

Call: ADSO URL with header details containing X-CSRF-TOKEN 

Get/set cookies from: ${header.set-cookie} 

Send Data to BW 

Request TSN (see first table) 

Prepare data load with Groovy-Script and set content type to JSON 

Send data including TSN (see first table) 

Close TSN request (see first table) 

Define Groovy Script  

Hint: Due to the example payload which contains all content – it was easy to generate the Groovy script and field mapping with support of SAP Joule.  

 


Runtime details in BTP Integration Suite 

StepIllustration
Get Request TSN 
Send Request including TSN 
Close Request   
Result in SAP BW/4HANAto be updated later...

Conclusion:

  • When data is posted into SAP BW/4HANA, it can be processed by Streaming Process chains automatically. See details in this blog post , and there part D.
  • For integration approaches of other cloud sources (mainly SAP) please refer to this blog post .
  • Use of AI to generate the mapping makes development easier and much faster - not only for this use case but in general!
  • A good documentation of API’s allows an improved speed of development. This blog post should provide exactly this.
  • This blog post will be updated when we learn more about delta-capability and performance in our dedicated scenario. We are just working on it, and we will keep you updated.
  • An Event-driven approach could be an option, if the business requires data in real time.

*Many thanks to Alexandros, Jingjiee and Christopher for their contribution.

entry.title: Expert Insights: Optimizing Performance in BW Remote Conversion Projects
entry.content:
With these proactive steps, your Remote Conversion project will not only perform better but also ensure a smoother migration experience.

Adjusting Package Sizes

Migrating in smaller chunks can often lead to excessive monitor entries and delays. Instead:

  • Set package sizes from 20MB to 120MB. This adjustment reduces the number of entries in the CNVLTRL_TASK table, improving system performance and monitor availability.

Leveraging Parallel Reads

Parallel reads help eliminate bottlenecks during data migration. To achieve this:

  • Try to use the first field as the parallel read field: If this is not possible, create a secondary index on the selected field. (for non HANA DBs).
  • Check the CNVLTRL_PORTION table to make sure that the portions are equally distributed: If not, use another field.

Use multiple clusters

Aim for about 20 clusters by adjusting the cluster size.

  • Use Simulation mode: Utilize it to identify the optimal size for clusters before initiating the creation.
  • Node distribution: Ensure that clusters are evenly distributed across nodes on both source and target systems.

Use Filters Strategically

Filters can reduce the data volume during migration, significantly improving performance.

  • Use fields like fiscal year or company code as filters.
  • For more control, add filter entries through transaction CNVLTRL_FILTER.
  • Apply these filters before running "Create Sequence to Generate and Execute Reports".
  • Ensure that your filtering strategy aligns with the table's structure and business logic.

Pre-Partitioning for Large Tables

For E- and F-tables with over 2 billion records:

  • Pre-partition these tables on the target system based on known entry counts.
  • This minimizes the risk of performance degradation during data writes.

Schedule Delta Merge Jobs

Large tables can significantly slow down migrations if not handled properly. Schedule delta merge jobs every two hours on the target system during the insert phase.

Key areas to focus on include:

  • Cluster tables.
  • Big tables.
  • CNVLTR_TASK table.
  • Log mode changes: Switch the log mode to "overwrite" on the target system to reduce log storage issues.
